task: I’m putting together a short post-production checklist for a freelance editor before picture lock, and I want it to catch the stuff that usually slips through. Can you help me draft one that’s practical and not overkill?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a practical, not-overkill **pre-picture-lock post-production checklist** for a freelance editor. It’s aimed at catching the common misses without turning into a giant paperwork exercise.

---

# Pre-Picture Lock Checklist for Freelance Editor

## 1) Timeline / Edit Integrity
- [ ] Confirm latest project version is open
- [ ] Check all sequences are in the correct order
- [ ] Verify no stray clips, temp cuts, or unused sequences are left in the timeline
- [ ] Make sure all trims, transitions, and speed changes are intentional
- [ ] Confirm no offline or missing media in the active sequence
- [ ] Check for duplicate shots or accidental repeats
- [ ] Review handles/cut points on all key edits

## 2) Picture Continuity
- [ ] Watch full cut for obvious continuity issues
- [ ] Check eyelines, screen direction, and jump cuts
- [ ] Confirm any intentional continuity breaks are noted
- [ ] Review graphics/inserts for timing and placement
- [ ] Make sure any split screens, overlays, or composites are correct

## 3) Audio
- [ ] Confirm temp audio is synced correctly
- [ ] Check for pops, clicks, clipped audio, or accidental mutes
- [ ] Verify dialogue is understandable throughout
- [ ] Review music and SFX levels for obvious problems
- [ ] Make sure any placeholder audio is labeled or flagged
- [ ] Check that room tone or ambience doesn’t cut unnaturally

## 4) Graphics / Text
- [ ] Spellcheck all on-screen text
- [ ] Verify names, titles, dates, locations, and captions
- [ ] Check lower thirds for consistency and safe placement
- [ ] Confirm fonts, sizes, and styles are consistent
- [ ] Make sure any legal or required text is included
- [ ] Check for typos in end cards, credits, and callouts

## 5) Color / Visual Consistency
- [ ] Check for obvious color shifts between shots
- [ ] Verify exposure, white balance, and skin tones are consistent enough for lock
- [ ] Make sure temp grades don’t create distracting mismatches
- [ ] Confirm any VFX shots are reasonably blended into the cut

## 6) Technical / Deliverable Readiness
- [ ] Confirm sequence frame rate matches project specs
- [ ] Check resolution and aspect ratio
- [ ] Verify safe margins/title safe if needed
- [ ] Confirm no missing fonts, plugins, or linked assets
- [ ] Make sure exports will include the correct tracks/layers
- [ ] Verify file naming is clear and versioned properly

## 7) Client / Review Notes
- [ ] Review all outstanding notes and confirm they’re addressed or flagged
- [ ] Mark any intentional unresolved items clearly
- [ ] Identify anything that still needs client approval before lock
- [ ] Keep a short list of open questions for the producer/client

## 8) Final Watchthrough
- [ ] Watch the cut start to finish without stopping
- [ ] Check for any last-minute mistakes after the final export
- [ ] Compare export against timeline for sync, text, and audio issues
- [ ] Save a backup of the locked version

---

## Optional “Quick Pass” Version
If you want this even leaner, use these five checks before picture lock:

- [ ] No missing media or accidental timeline errors
- [ ] Audio is synced, clean, and understandable
- [ ] Graphics/text are spelled correctly and placed properly
- [ ] Cut is visually consistent and free of obvious continuity issues
- [ ] Export matches project specs and has been fully reviewed

If you want, I can also turn this into:
1. a **one-page client-friendly checklist**,  
2. a **checkbox table for Google Docs/Notion**, or  
3. a **more detailed version for narrative/commercial/editorial work**.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty solid, but it’s a little broad. I’d want a few more things that actually bite people at lock — like missing slates, temp VFX still in the cut, mixed frame rates, bad channel mapping, and whether the export matches the delivery spec exactly. Also worth separating **picture lock prep** from **final delivery QC** so it doesn’t get muddy.

If you tighten it up, I’d make it more like:
- timeline clean
- audio checked
- graphics/text verified
- color/VFX reviewed
- spec/delivery confirmed
- notes cleared
- final watchthrough

And under each one, include the common gotchas, not every possible issue. That’s the part people actually miss.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter version that separates **picture lock prep** from **final delivery QC** and focuses on the common misses.

---

# Pre-Picture Lock Checklist for Freelance Editor

## 1) Timeline Clean
- [ ] Latest sequence/version is open
- [ ] No stray selects, temp cuts, or old versions in the active timeline
- [ ] No missing media, offline clips, or accidental gaps
- [ ] No duplicate shots, repeated sections, or leftover placeholders
- [ ] Slates/countdowns are present where needed
- [ ] Sequence is in the correct order and duration is expected
- [ ] Mixed frame rates / conform issues have been checked
- [ ] Handles/cut points are acceptable for finishing

## 2) Audio Checked
- [ ] Temp audio is synced correctly
- [ ] Dialogue is clear and not accidentally muted or clipped
- [ ] Pops, clicks, distortion, and bad edits are cleaned up or flagged
- [ ] Music/SFX levels are reasonable for lock
- [ ] Channel mapping is correct for the intended export
- [ ] No stray tracks, hidden mics, or unintended audio sources
- [ ] Placeholder audio is clearly labeled

## 3) Graphics / Text Verified
- [ ] All on-screen text is spelled correctly
- [ ] Names, titles, dates, locations, and captions are correct
- [ ] Lower thirds are consistent and safely placed
- [ ] End cards, slates, and credits are correct
- [ ] Any legal text or required supers are included
- [ ] No temp graphics, placeholder text, or unfinished titles remain

## 4) Color / VFX Reviewed
- [ ] Obvious shot-to-shot color mismatches are checked
- [ ] Temp grades aren’t creating distracting inconsistencies
- [ ] Skin tones/exposure are acceptable across the cut
- [ ] Temp VFX are identified and either approved for lock or flagged
- [ ] No unfinished comps, green screen holds, or obvious placeholder effects remain
- [ ] Any shots needing finishing are clearly noted

## 5) Spec / Delivery Confirmed
- [ ] Sequence frame rate matches the delivery spec
- [ ] Resolution and aspect ratio match the required format
- [ ] Export settings match the exact delivery spec
- [ ] Audio layout/channel mapping matches spec
- [ ] Correct codec/container is selected
- [ ] Safe margins/title safe are correct if required
- [ ] File naming/versioning is correct

## 6) Notes Cleared
- [ ] All editor notes have been addressed, answered, or flagged
- [ ] Any unresolved items are clearly marked
- [ ] Client/producer questions are tracked separately
- [ ] Nothing important is left ambiguous before lock

## 7) Final Watchthrough
- [ ] Watch the cut start to finish without stopping
- [ ] Check for last-minute issues in picture, audio, graphics, and timing
- [ ] Confirm slates, supers, and end cards appear correctly
- [ ] Compare the export against the timeline for sync, text, and audio issues
- [ ] Save a backup of the locked version

---

# Final Delivery QC
Use this only after picture lock, before sending the master.

- [ ] Export matches the approved picture lock exactly
- [ ] No unintended changes from the locked timeline
- [ ] Frame rate, resolution, codec, and aspect ratio match delivery spec
- [ ] Audio channels are mapped correctly
- [ ] Slates, captions, graphics, and end cards appear as expected
- [ ] No missing frames, glitches, or export errors
- [ ] Spot-check start, middle, and end of the file
- [ ] Confirm file name, version, and delivery package are correct

---

If you want, I can also turn this into:
- a **super-short 1-page version**
- a **checkbox table for Notion/Google Docs**
- or a **version tailored for docs/commercial/narrative work**

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is good. That split helps a lot — lock prep vs delivery QC is the right way to keep it from turning into one blurry catch-all.

And the gotchas you added are the actual ones: slates, temp VFX, mixed frame rates, channel mapping, exact spec match. That’s the stuff people miss when they’re moving too fast.

I’d use this as-is.
